text.title: Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank pipeline
text.summary: Why a wide first pass plus a precise rerank dominates a single embedding-only top-K, and how to size the candidate window.

section.title: Why two stages
section.p: Embedding similarity is fast (one vector dot-product) but coarse — it cannot model query-chunk interaction. A cross-encoder is slow (one Transformer pass per pair) but precise — it sees the (query, chunk) jointly. The two-stage pipeline uses each model where it is strongest: embeddings for recall over the corpus, cross-encoder for precision over the candidate list.

rule.statement: Retrieve 50-200 candidates with embeddings (recall pass) and rerank with a cross-encoder before passing top 5-10 to the reasoner; never feed raw embedding top-K directly to a strong-model reasoner.
rule.rationale: Pinecone and ZeroEntropy 2025 benchmarks report 10-20 pp precision@10 gains on enterprise retrieval suites; the same studies show that windows below 50 leave recall on the table while windows above 200 add latency without quality.

example.code: candidates = vector_store.search(query, top_k=75) # recall pass scored = reranker.rank(query, [c.text for c in candidates]) # precision pass top = [c for c, s in zip(candidates, scored) if s > 0.3][:8] answer = strong_model.respond(query, context=top)
example.note: Wide first pass, narrow second pass; reasoner sees only the top 8 high-precision chunks.

example.code: top = vector_store.search(query, top_k=8) answer = strong_model.respond(query, context=top)
example.note: Reasoner gets 8 surface-similar chunks that may miss the actually relevant ones; precision@8 is ~10-20 pp below the two-stage pipeline.

section.reference: Pinecone — Two-stage retrieval and rerankers in RAG.

section.title: Sizing the candidate window
section.p: Candidate window K is a knob with measurable cost and quality. Too small and recall is lost; too large and the reranker pays cost on noise.

rule.statement: Sweep K ∈ {25, 50, 75, 100, 150} on a labelled eval set; pick the smallest K where precision@10 plateaus.
rule.rationale: Most production corpora plateau between K=50 and K=100. Going past the plateau spends reranker latency without recall gain; staying below it under-retrieves rare-but-relevant chunks.

antipattern.description: Fixed K=10 inherited from a single-stage RAG quickstart.
antipattern.reason: K=10 collapses the two-stage pattern back to a single stage; the reranker has nothing to rerank.

section.reference: Reference pipeline with configurable candidate window and threshold.

see-also.link: How to choose between Cohere, BGE, and Voyage rerankers.
